Q1: I usually organized my study routine around blocks of time between my extracurriculars and classes. Being a kinesthetic learner, I'd often schedule study sessions where I could physically work through problems or practice applications, not just read. I'd dedicate about 19 hours a week, spreading it across most days, and I always tried to have my materials ready the night before so I could jump right in. I found that having a consistent spot in the library where I could spread out my notes and move around a bit really helped me focus.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up sometimes. I tried to keep a steady rhythm—studying a bit each day—but when big assignments were due, I'd definitely shift my schedule to accommodate more intensive sessions. The consistency helped me stay on top of my 20 online courses, but flexibility was necessary when things piled up. I think having some structure while allowing for adjustments worked best for me.
Q3: Honestly, I'd log into the LMS most often to check for new announcements or to see if any deadlines were approaching. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch lecture videos or check discussion boards to see what my classmates were saying. It became almost automatic—like checking my phone in the morning. If I knew there was content I needed to review before a practical session, that would definitely prompt me to log in too.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became part of my morning routine, right after breakfast. I think it happened because I realized I'd miss important updates if I didn't check regularly, and the course structure kind of trained me to expect new content at certain times. It felt natural after a while, like brushing my teeth. The high engagement just became a habit I didn't have to think about anymore.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace—I watched nearly 300 minutes of content. I also found the discussion posts helpful because seeing how other students interpreted concepts gave me new perspectives. For my kinesthetic learning style, any interactive elements or simulations were gold because they let me actually do something rather than just passively absorb information.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skipped over supplementary reading materials, especially long PDFs that felt more theoretical than practical. If something didn't seem directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd put it on the back burner and often never return to it. I also didn't engage as much with some of the optional quizzes because they felt less urgent than the graded work. Time was limited, so I had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to what I'd be assessed on or when it helped me actually understand something I'd been struggling with. If I could apply what I learned immediately—like practicing a technique or solving a real problem—that made it feel valuable. I also appreciated activities that let me interact with peers because learning felt less isolating that way.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, if that makes sense. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew they'd affect my grade, so I prioritized them no matter what. Optional stuff I'd do when I had extra time or when I was genuinely curious, but there was always this nagging feeling that I should focus on what was mandatory first. Sometimes the optional content was actually more interesting, which was frustrating.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study on my tablet during my commute or when I didn't have reliable internet. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd sketch out concepts and make diagrams—that hands-on process really helped things stick for me. Sometimes I'd use external YouTube videos or textbooks from the library to supplement what we covered in class. A lot of my learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times when I'd print out materials and study them at a café or in the park, working through problems by hand. I'd also form study groups with classmates where we'd meet in person and work through concepts together—none of that showed up in the LMS. Sometimes my best learning happened when I was physically manipulating objects or walking through processes out loud, which obviously leaves no digital trace.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then I'd revisit it multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward. It was more convenient to have things saved locally, especially when I wanted to study in places without great Wi-Fi or during transit.
Q12: There were definitely moments, usually mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. I'd feel this medium-level stress building, and my motivation would dip even though I knew I needed to push through. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me reframe those difficult periods and find ways to cope. When I was juggling extracurriculars and multiple courses, it was harder to give each course the attention it deserved.
Q13: Fatigue was a real factor, especially after long days with extracurricular activities. When I was exhausted, I'd still log into the LMS but maybe just skim through things rather than deeply engage. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my usage patterns—sometimes I'd have to choose between thorough studying and just getting something done. Confusion occasionally made me avoid certain modules until I felt mentally ready to tackle them.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, or I'd scroll through content without really absorbing it. It's like being physically present but mentally checked out. Those moments usually happened when I was tired or stressed about other things. I'd realize later that I needed to go back and actually engage with the material properly.
Q15: I definitely postponed some of the more challenging assignments, even when I knew they were important. I think it was a combination of feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Sometimes the fear of not doing well made me avoid things, which is ironic because waiting only made it worse. The CBT techniques I learned helped me recognize this pattern and push through it more often.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me—they created this urgency that pushed me to prioritize and focus. I'd often see my LMS activity spike in the days leading up to a due date. I tried to avoid last-minute cramming, but sometimes deadlines were the push I needed to actually complete something I'd been putting off. They structured my week in a way that kept me accountable.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I received constructive comments on assignments, I felt more motivated to improve and engage more deeply with the next task. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me want to maintain that standard. If feedback was delayed or vague, I sometimes felt a bit lost about how to improve, which could dampen my enthusiasm temporarily.
Q18: The course structure really shaped how I participated. Having clear modules with defined learning outcomes helped me know what to focus on each week. The regular release of new content kept me coming back to the LMS consistently. When the structure was logical and built on previous concepts, I felt more confident navigating the material. It gave me a roadmap that made the workload feel manageable.
Q19: Interactive elements and practical applications made engagement much easier for me as a kinesthetic learner. When courses included hands-on activities or simulations, I was way more invested. On the flip side, courses that were heavily text-based or required lots of passive reading were harder to stay engaged with. Clear navigation and well-organized content also made a big difference in how willing I was to explore.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly or reading discussions because they're confused, but that effort doesn't translate into completed assignments. It could also be anxiety about submitting work that isn't perfect, or life circumstances that allow for browsing but not focused work time. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show in my LMS logs, and I performed reasonably well. Someone could download materials once and study them extensively elsewhere, or they might be efficient learners who don't need to revisit content multiple times. Prior knowledge or strong study skills could also mean less time needed on the platform to achieve good results.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns of access—when students log in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. They're good at capturing digital footprints and identifying students who might be completely disengaged. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, collaborative learning that happens in person, and the mental and emotional aspects of learning. They can't tell if someone is truly understanding or just going through the motions.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actively wrestling with the material—not just passively consuming it but thinking critically, asking questions, and trying to apply what I'm learning. It's about feeling connected to the content and seeing its relevance to my goals. True engagement involves curiosity and a desire to understand deeply, not just to complete tasks. It's both intellectual and emotional investment in my learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the physical study techniques I use, like creating hands-on models or walking through problems out loud. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions I have in person or the way I process information while doing other activities. My emotional journey—the stress I managed, the CBT sessions that helped me stay on track, the moments of genuine excitement about learning—none of that appears in the data. The human side of my engagement is invisible to the system.
